3: In 2011, 9 million tons of used materials ended up as waste in Denmark. Of these 9 million tons, 61% was recycled, 29% combusted for combined heat and electricity production and 6% were disposed of at landfills. According to the National resource action plan for waste management (Danish Government, 2013a,b; DEPA, 2014), the loss of valuable resources associated with the 35% waste com- busted or landfilled, is to be transformed into a closed loop circular flow; with the ultimate aim of transforming the Danish society into a zero waste society (Danish Government, 2013b).

The historical trend in the Danish waste management system is an increasing amount of waste being combusted with energy pro- duction (Nielsen et al., 2015). In 2010, approximately 54% of the total municipal solid waste (MSW) was collected and transported for combustion at Waste to Energy (WtE) plants (Kjxer, 2013). The development has been influenced by the former Danish govern- mental waste strategies (Nielsen et al., 2015) and action plans defining waste combustion with energy production as a preferable alternative to landfilling. The latter due to energy recovery (Danish Government, 2003, 2009), whilst capturing or destroying harmful substances through the implementation of air pollution control devices and effective waste combustion technologies (IPPC, 2006). Besides energy recovery, minerals and chemical content of the combustion residues may to some extent be recovered (DEPA, 2013; Pizzol et al., 2013). However, in the recent years it has become clear that there may be a more sustainable and efficient way of producing energy while recovering minerals, nutrients and

chemicals (Thomsen et al., 2015). Presently, about 80% of Danish household waste is combusted whereas the goal of the resource plan is to sort out and reuse 50% of the household waste by 2022 (DEPA, 2013).

In addition to the Resource plan for Waste Management, the Danish Government adopted a new long-term plan for the estab- lishment of increased biogas production from manure (Agreement on Green Growth, 2009, 2010). According to the 2020 goal, 50% of all the manure has to be co-digested with suitable feedstocks prior to use as biofertilizer within the agricultural sector (DEPA, 2014; Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009). A support scheme restricts the use of energy crops, which cannot exceed 25% and by 2018, the maximum co-digestion of energy crops is restricted to 12% of the ingestate feedstock (DEA, 2012). Biowaste represents a suitable alternative feedstock with no restrictions on co-digestion at biogas plants ac- cording to the support scheme of the Danish Energy Agency (DEA, 2012). The latter, supported by the Resource Action Plan for Waste Management, points towards diverting household waste away from combustion towards co-digestion with manure for combined biogas and biofertilizer production (DEPA, 2014).

The organic fraction of household waste (OFHW) represents an organic biomass flow originating directly or indirectly from the agricultural sector. Therefore, a separate collection of organic household waste, allocated for co-digestion with sludge or manure from dairy farms, may constitute a future closed loop organic mineral and nutrient cycle (Jensen et al., 2015; Thomsen et al., 2015). An increasingly circular bioresource management system as described above may be a feasible way of preventing the risk of soil carbon degradation; i.e. by utilizing the volatile or easily degradable carbon for biogas production prior to returning a sta- bilized biofertilizer product, i.e. digestate with high recalcitrant carbon content, to agricultural land (Pognani et al., 2009). This cycle, returning biofertilizers for long-term conservation of soil fertility and carbon stock, could alleviate one of the environmental challenges associated with the increased intensification of the agricultural sector.

Several papers have been published about the comparison be- tween different waste scenarios management. In particular a large number of LCA's have been performed on waste management op- tions, comparing different waste management and technology systems like landfilling, composting, anaerobic digestion and/or incineration (Arena et al., 2003; Baky and Eriksson, 2003; Eriksson et al., 2007; Rigamonti et al., 2009; Wittmaier et al., 2009; Jensen et al., 2016). Münster and Lund (2010) revealed that in Denmark, biogas and thermal gasification technologies are interesting alter- natives to waste incineration and it is recommended to support the use of biogas based on manure and organic waste. However the feasibility of diverting OFHW away from incineration towards biogas plants is still not fully evaluated regarding, e.g. changes in the heat and electricity production compared to the demand. Furthermore, challenges regarding the quality of the digestate and its direct use as a biofertilizer product or as feedstock for other biobased transformation activities is still not fully explored (Pizzol et al., 2014; Seghetta et al., 2016a, b; Thomsen et al., 2012).

The present paper presents an assessment of the environmental performance of an increased circular bioresource management system, considering Denmark as a case study. The OFHW is real- located from combustion to co-digestion at manure- and sludge- based biogas plants producing energy and natural organic bio- fertilizer products. This study evaluates the consequences of implementation of the goals defined within the National resource action plan for Waste management considering a 7% reduction in the total amount of waste combusted at a Danish WtE plant, cor- responding to 33% of the total OFHW produced inside the system boundary (Fig. The reallocated amount of OFHW corresponds to

a 22% reduction in municipal solid waste going to waste combus- tion. In the present paper, Denmark is taken as case study that may be considered a significant reference model for all the regional systems at the macro and micro level who are interested in assessing the environmental performances resulting from diverting waste from incineration towards biogas and biofertilizer produc- tion. The study is one of several technology transitions increasing the use of waste as a resource in a future circularity economy (Lee et al., 2014; Seghetta et al., 2016b), in accordance to the European circular economy action plan and associated legislations (COM/ 2015/0614 final).

Section 2 provides a description of the reference (RS) and alternative (AS) bioresource management systems, together with the goal, scope and functional unit of the systems. Section 3 pro- vides a detailed description of life cycle inventory data quantifying the energy and resource flows of the RS and AS. Section 4 presents the results of the comparative bioresource Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) and Section 4 discusses and concludes on the study.

chemicals (Thomsen et al., 2015). Presently, about 80% of Danish household waste is combusted whereas the goal of the resource plan is to sort out and reuse 50% of the household waste by 2022 (DEPA, 2013).

In addition to the Resource plan for Waste Management, the Danish Government adopted a new long-term plan for the estab- lishment of increased biogas production from manure (Agreement on Green Growth, 2009, 2010). According to the 2020 goal, 50% of all the manure has to be co-digested with suitable feedstocks prior to use as biofertilizer within the agricultural sector (DEPA, 2014; Holm-Nielsen et al., 2009). A support scheme restricts the use of energy crops, which cannot exceed 25% and by 2018, the maximum co-digestion of energy crops is restricted to 12% of the ingestate feedstock (DEA, 2012). Therefore, a separate collection of organic household waste, allocated for co-digestion with sludge or manure from dairy farms, may constitute a future closed loop organic mineral and nutrient cycle (Jensen et al., 2015; Thomsen et al., 2015). An increasingly circular bioresource management system as described above may be a feasible way of preventing the risk of soil carbon degradation; i.e. by utilizing the volatile or easily degradable carbon for biogas production prior to returning a sta- bilized biofertilizer product, i.e. digestate with high recalcitrant carbon content, to agricultural land (Pognani et al., 2009). This cycle, returning biofertilizers for long-term conservation of soil fertility and carbon stock, could alleviate one of the environmental challenges associated with the increased intensification of the agricultural sector.

Several papers have been published about the comparison be- tween different waste scenarios management. However the feasibility of diverting OFHW away from incineration towards biogas plants is still not fully evaluated regarding, e.g. changes in the heat and electricity production compared to the demand. a 22% reduction in municipal solid waste going to waste combus- tion. In the present paper, Denmark is taken as case study that may be considered a significant reference model for all the regional systems at the macro and micro level who are interested in assessing the environmental performances resulting from diverting waste from incineration towards biogas and biofertilizer produc- tion. The study is one of several technology transitions increasing the use of waste as a resource in a future circularity economy (Lee et al., 2014; Seghetta et al., 2016b), in accordance to the European circular economy action plan and associated legislations (COM/ 2015/0614 final).

10: The LCA study was performed in accordance with the interna- tional standards ISO 14040-44 (ISO, 2006a; ISO, 2006b). The defi- nition of system boundaries and goal of the LCA were designed in order to include the described changes in biowaste flows, treat- ment and conversion technologies. The calculation was performed using SimaPro 8.0.4 software (PRé Consultants, 2008) and the in- tegrated inventory Ecoinvent v2 (Frischknecht et al., 2007). The impact assessment was performed utilizing the life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) methodology ReCiPe V1.06 at midpoint.

The study aims to evaluate the changes in resource efficiency upon transforming the RS into the alternative biowaste manage- ment system (AS) including a comparative assessment of the associated impacts on the environment and human health considering the impact categories: Climate Change (CC), Fossil Depletion (FD), Terrestrial Ecotoxicity (TE), Human Toxicity (HT), Freshwater and Marine Eutrophication (FE and ME).
11: The goal of this study is to compare different strategies of bio- waste management. The study takes point of departure in a Danish case study. The former Danish strategy of Waste to Energy (WtE) production is quantified in the RS (Danish Government, 2003, 2009, 2010), portraying the RS management of OFHW, manure and sludge in 2011. The AS describes the bioresource management system for the year 2020 in which the goals of Na- tional resource action plan for waste management have been reached; in this paper obtained by reallocating 21% of the total amount of fresh weight household waste going to combustion (DEPA, 2014). This corresponds to 33% of the dry weight OFHW reallocated to co-digestion at manure- and sludge-based biogas plants.
12: The case study considers a territorial area comprising 32 mu- nicipalities located in North Zealand, of which 19 municipalities delivers their municipal solid waste for combustion at the WtE, Vestforbraending. In the RS, the manure produced on the livestock farms, is spread directly on agricultural soil. Biogas is produced from sludge at eight wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) receiving wastewater from the collective sewer system crossing the 32 municipalities. The biowaste flows included in the system boundaries consist of manure, sludge from eight WWTPs and the

The system boundary of the biowaste management system of the consequential LCA includes the part of the reference biowaste management system that is subjected to change in the alternative biowaste management system.

In fresh weight, the system includes 154 Gg of OFHW (Vestforbraending green account, 2011) from 32 municipalities, 170 Gg fresh weight of manure and 138 Gg fresh weight of sludge being digested at the 8 WWTPs. System boundaries for the fore- ground system were defines as follows.

For the biowaste input, i.e. manure, OFHW and sludge, their contribution to the system needs to be specified. Manure is considered an unexploited resource dependent on livestock pro- duction, therefore impacts related to production of manure are attributed 100% to livestock and therefore kept outside the system boundaries, while the use phase of manure, i.e. emissions to air and soil during storage and spreading, are included in the system boundary. Transportation of manure from livestock farms to the biogas plants and back to the agricultural cropland are included in the biomass transport system.

The production of waste is excluded from the system boundaries consistently with normal practice in treatment-LCAs (Gentil et al., 2011). However, the system includes the impacts related to waste management, i.e. emissions to air and soil, in the reference and alternative waste management systems, i.e. combustion and anaerobic co-digestion with consequent spreading of digestate.
For sludge delivered by the WWTP to the sludge-based biogas

plants, the impacts are attributed 100% to the treated water since this is what the communities pay for. However, the emissions associated with anaerobic digestion of sludge and further spreading of digestate are included in the system boundary. The WtE plant, which is the main bioresource management strategy of the RS, is still included in the AS, as it is still part of the bioresource management system in our study. Only the OFHW is subjected to a change upon transforming the RS into the AS and therefore only this fraction is included in the consequential LCA. Input-output data for the WtE plant were assigned according to the dry mass of the combusted waste fractions. Only 33% of the dry weight OFHW (or 7% of the total amount of dry weight waste combusted) is treated via co-digestion at the sludge- and manure- based biogas plants in the AS, instead of being treated in the WtE plans as in the RS.

The system does not consider the impacts associated to con- struction of the infrastructure to deliver heat and electricity as well as losses in the grid during distribution. Five new biogas plants are planned in order to convert manure into biogas prior to applying the digestate to agricultural cropland.

The system boundary of the biowaste management system of the consequential LCA includes the part of the reference biowaste management system that is subjected to change in the alternative biowaste management system.

In fresh weight, the system includes 154 Gg of OFHW (Vestforbraending green account, 2011) from 32 municipalities, 170 Gg fresh weight of manure and 138 Gg fresh weight of sludge being digested at the 8 WWTPs. System boundaries for the fore- ground system were defines as follows.

For the biowaste input, i.e. manure, OFHW and sludge, their contribution to the system needs to be specified. Manure is considered an unexploited resource dependent on livestock pro- duction, therefore impacts related to production of manure are attributed 100% to livestock and therefore kept outside the system boundaries, while the use phase of manure, i.e. emissions to air and soil during storage and spreading, are included in the system boundary. Transportation of manure from livestock farms to the biogas plants and back to the agricultural cropland are included in the biomass transport system.

The production of waste is excluded from the system boundaries consistently with normal practice in treatment-LCAs (Gentil et al., 2011). However, the system includes the impacts related to waste management, i.e. emissions to air and soil, in the reference and alternative waste management systems, i.e. combustion and anaerobic co-digestion with consequent spreading of digestate.
For sludge delivered by the WWTP to the sludge-based biogas

plants, the impacts are attributed 100% to the treated water since this is what the communities pay for. However, the emissions associated with anaerobic digestion of sludge and further spreading of digestate are included in the system boundary. The WtE plant, which is the main bioresource management strategy of the RS, is still included in the AS, as it is still part of the bioresource management system in our study. Only the OFHW is subjected to a change upon transforming the RS into the AS and therefore only this fraction is included in the consequential LCA. Input-output data for the WtE plant were assigned according to the dry mass of the combusted waste fractions. Only 33% of the dry weight OFHW (or 7% of the total amount of dry weight waste combusted) is treated via co-digestion at the sludge- and manure- based biogas plants in the AS, instead of being treated in the WtE plans as in the RS.

The system does not consider the impacts associated to con- struction of the infrastructure to deliver heat and electricity as well as losses in the grid during distribution. Five new biogas plants are planned in order to convert manure into biogas prior to applying the digestate to agricultural cropland.

In the RS, the manure produced on the livestock farms, is spread directly on agricultural soil. Biogas is produced from sludge at eight wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) receiving wastewater from the collective sewer system crossing the 32 municipalities. 17: In order to consider different types of biomass we selected a functional unit based on the geographical region of North Zealand (2512 km2) corresponding to 132 Gg dry weight (dw) biowaste managed within a time horizon of 1 year (Fig. 1). The amount and type of biowaste included in the analysis constitutes 70.0 Gg dw of OFHW, 20.6 Gg dw of manure and 41.5 Gg dw of ingestate sewage sludge.

18: The RS represents a situation where no change has occurred to the existing waste management system in 2020 compared to 2011. The OFHW and sludge production are kept constant since no sig- nificant increase is expected (Kjxer, 2013; Eurostat, 2009; Bioenergy, 2014). The AS represents an increased exploitation of existing biowaste for biogas production from anaerobic co- digestion of OFHW at manure-based biogas plants in the ratio of 0.25 mass units of OFHW per unit dw manure (DEPA, 2013) at five manure-based biogas plants within the geographical system boundary, at suitable locations according to municipal plans (Fig. 1). Furthermore, co-digestion of 0.41 mass units of OFHW per unit of dw sludge (Bolzonella et al., 2006) at biogas plants located at 8 existing WWTPs within the system boundary. The resource flows from input biowaste according to types, energy conversion tech- nologies and final products for the RS and AS is visualized in Fig. 2.

The diverting of OFHW away from waste combustion towards biogasification corresponds to a 33% reduction in the OFHW incinerated in 2020 compared to the reference year 2011. For manure, an increase in the amount biogasified from zero to 20573 Mg in AS is implemented, i.e. corresponding to 84% of the total manure produced inside the system boundary.
Of the 23 Gg OFHW removed from the WtE plant, 22% is real- located for manure-based biogas plants, while 78% is added as external carbon to the ingestate at the 8 sludge-based biogas plants.

The separation of OFHW and IFHW in this study is presumed to occur at the household level by means of a source separated waste collection (DEPA, 2013). As such, it is assumed that the AS does not require extra consumption of energy and material as would be the case for a centralized waste separation system.

20: The Life Cycle Inventory involves five processes: municipal solid

waste management, manure management, wastewater manage- ment, anaerobic co-digestion of the biomass and transport. A detailed description of the input data and assumptions adopted to quantify each process is provided in Supplementary Material, Appendix A.
21: Life cycle impact assessment of the waste management system was performed using the ReCiPe LCIA method at midpoint level (Goedkoop et al., 2009). We selected six impact categories ac- cording to the potential impacts associated with waste manage- ment. Besides the climate change-related impacts (CC), the waste management is associated with certain environmental quality is- sues leading to potential impacts on terrestrial ecotoxicity (TE) upon substitution of mineral fertilizers and concurrent reduction in the impact category fossil depletion (FD). Likewise, the net air emissions of the RS and AS influence the impact categories human toxicity (HT) and finally the resource efficiency of the system impact categories freshwater and marine eutrophication (FE and ME). The selected impact categories included in the LCA are listed below:

HT; quantified in units of 1,4-dichlorobenzene equivalents, [kg 1,4-DB eq.] 
TE; quantified in kg 1,4-dichlorobenzene equivalents, [kg 1,4-DB eq.] 
FE; quantified in kg phosphorous equivalents, [kg P eq.] 
ME; quantified as kg nitrogen equivalents, [kg eq.]</list>
22: In this section the environmental performance of the two bio- resource management scenarios are presented (Table 1). We also show results of each impact category aggregated in twelve pro- cesses (Fig. 3): WtE - Process and emissions, WtE - Avoided elec- tricity, WtE - Avoided heat, AD1 - Process and emissions, AD1 Avoided electricity, AD1 - Avoided heat, AD1 - Avoided mineral fertilizers production and use, AD2 - Process and emissions, AD2 Avoided electricity, AD2 - Avoided heat, AD2 - Avoided mineral fertilizers production and use, Transport. The impact value for each category is provided in Supplementary Materials (Table S12). Moreover, in Section 3.2 a sensitivity analysis is presented.
23: Table 1 shows that for both bioresource management systems, net negative values are observed for the impact categories CC, FD and FE. Such net negative values of environmental performance represents environmental benefits (or avoided impacts) delivered by both the RS and more pronounced for the AS. Four of the six assessed impact categories, i.e.CC, FD, FE and ME, shows improved environmental performance of the alternative bioresource man- agement strategy compared to the RS (Table 1). The increased negative values for the AS on the categories CC, FD, FE and ME constitutes the added value, i.e. benefits, of the AS in terms of avoided impacts. For the two impact categories HT and TE, the alternative bioresource management scenario, show a reduction in environmental performance, i.e. increased impacts, expressed through increased negative impacts on human health and the terrestrial environment.
24: The environmental performance of the AS shows a reduction of 10% in the system level greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. The reduced GHG emissions is explained by a net increase in the total green energy production of 16% in the AS resulting from an increased production of biogas from manure co-digested with OFHW and an additional increase in methane yield in the sludge- based biogas production upon co-digestion with external carbon in terms of OFHW.

The increased methane emissions from biogas production, i.e. fugitive methane emissions during process and storage, in the AS, are insignificant compared to the increased substitution of fossil energy production. Especially, the utilisation of the unexplored methane potential in raw manure in RS results in a significant increased substitution of fossil-based energy production in AS. The AS shows a total increase in the electricity production from biogas of 39% and a decrease in heat production of 8%.

Thanks to the increased closed loop circulation of the nutrients contained in the biowaste in the AS, a reduction in emissions from mineral fertilizer production of 26% is obtained compared to the reference scenario. At system level, the substitution of mineral

28: For the impact category FD the same patterns as for CC may be observed. In both scenarios, the energy production from the different biomass conversion processes decreases the systems' fossil energy consumption. This results in an avoided depletion of fossil resources in both scenarios. Due to the reduction in the OFHW combusted at the WtE plant, the heat and electricity production delivered by the WtE plant decrease in the AS. At the same time heat and electricity production at the biogas plants increase due to implementation of co-digestion of organic waste at both sludge and manure-based biogas plants. As mentioned above, the ratio electricity-heat does not change proportionally to the decrease in the combustion of OFHW. Heat production in the AS is 8% less compared to the RS, while the electricity production in the AS in- creases 39%.
29: When evaluating the contribution to HT in the two scenarios, it is important to remember that for the WtE plant, only the fraction of air emissions and effluents originating from the allocated amount of OFHW, is included in the system boundary. The main activity contributing with positive values is the application of digestate derived fertilizer products, from manure-based and sludge-based biogas production (AD), on agricultural land which is increasing 90% in the AS compared to the RS. The compound causing this increase is zinc constituting 98% of the total increase in HT and the reason is the high content of zinc in the OFHW (Sup- plementary material, Tables S8 and S9). The main reason for the higher contribution from sludge-based digestate compared to manure-based digestate is due to a higher fraction of the OFHW, i.e. 43% of the dry weight ingestate at the sludge-based biogas plants, while the OFHW constitutes only 25% of the dry weight ingestate at the manure-based biogas plants.

As described above, the increase in the system level impact on human health is dominated by the final disposal of the residue digestate from anaerobic digestion of the biomass. Fig. 3c shows that the spreading of the digestate in the AS results in an increased deterioration of the environmental condition and consequently an increase of HT due to oral intake of micropollutants which bio- concentrate in root crops (Pizzol al., 2014). Among the substances affecting the result from substituting the spreading of mineral fertilizers products with digestate as fertilizer product, zinc con- stitutes 74% and 67% of the total contribution to HT from respec- tively spreading of manure and sludge in the RS and 85% and 79% in the AS. Cadmium constitutes 20% and 24% of the total contribution from spreading of respectively manure and sludge as fertilizers in the RS, which reduces to 11% and 15% in the AS. If we look at the difference between the RS and the AS, 98% of the change in the total impact is caused by zinc, followed by lead, antimony and cadmium, each contributing less than 1% of the total.

The effect of air emissions during the waste combustion process is negligible in both scenarios (Pizzol et al., 2013). Slags and fly ashes are assumed disposed of at landfills or as subbase layer in asphalted secondary road (Birgisdóttir et al., 2007) and pollutants in such combustion residues are assumed immobilized and isolated

Results are visualised divided into twelve processes Transport (including transport of the OFHW to all plants located inside the system boundary), avoided fossil based heat and energy production due to production of green heat and electricity from manure-based biogas, AD1, sludge-based biogas, AD2, and combustion, WtE, avoided mineral fertilizer production due to substitution with digestate from AD1 and AD2. Lastly process and emissions from AD1 plants, AD2 plants and the WtE plant.

The considerations made for the HT are also valid for the TE where the AS present an overall higher positive value compared to the RS. The main contribution is from the spreading of the digestate (AD). The increase in the two scenarios is relative to the increase in biomass co-digested when including OFHW. The avoided emissions due to substituted mineral fertilizer does not counterbalance the positive values associated with emissions from the spreading of natural organic fertilizers, and net positive values are obtained for the total system. Compared to the HT the avoided emissions have a smaller effect on the overall impact. It should be noted that the LCA only considers emissions to air and water from the combustion plant, whereas the indirect emissions to soil via deposition of stack gas are not considered relevant in a one-year time horizon (Pizzol et al., 2013). Emissions to soil due to leakages from disposal of slags either in landfills or as subbase layer in asphalted roads is considered negligible (Birgisdóttir et al., 2007).

If we look at the difference between the RS and the AS, 98% of the change in the total impact is caused by zinc, followed by lead, antimony and cadmium, each contributing less than 1% of the total.

The effect of air emissions during the waste combustion process is negligible in both scenarios (Pizzol et al., 2013). Slags and fly ashes are assumed disposed of at landfills or as subbase layer in asphalted secondary road (Birgisdóttir et al., 2007) and pollutants in such combustion residues are assumed immobilized and isolated

34: The environmental performance of the AS increases compared to the RS corresponding to a net reduction in the contribution to

freshwater eutrophication level of 34%. An increase in emissions of P to the aquatic system is occurring via the increase in the total volume of digestate spread because of the co-digestion of OFHW. Such increase is, however, counterbalanced by the avoided pro- duction and spreading of mineral P fertilizers.

It is relevant to remember that the total amount of phosphorus spread on soil is not affected by the anaerobic digestion, meaning that the substitution ratio is assumed equal to 0.35 with and without biogasification of the manure (Niero et al., 2014). Lastly, the leaching coefficient, quantifying the emission of phosphorous to the freshwater systems, is assumed equal to 0.025 for both natural and mineral fertilizer products (Seghetta et al., 2016a). The reduc- tion in the eutrophication level is mainly caused by an increase in avoided production of mineral P fertilizer corresponding to the substituted amount of P in the co-digested OFHW. The latter resulting in a decreased loss of P during mining activities for the extraction of raw phosphate; i.e. positive impact at global level, that does not contribute to improved conditions of local freshwater as catchment area for surrounding agricultural land use areas (Jensen et al., 2015; Niero et al., 2014). The avoided fossil energy production, substituted by green electricity production from the WtE and biogas plants causes a reduction in eutrophication due to avoided loss of phosphorous associated to waste disposal from coal mining

36: For the impact category ME, net negative impacts in the RS transforms into net positive impacts in the AS; i.e. RS contributes to an increase in the eutrophication of marine waters, while the AS delivers a net reduction in the N emissions to marine waters (Supplementary material, Table S12). Therefore significant benefits, in terms of environmental restoration of the marine aquatic system upon changing the bioresource management system from the RS to the AS results.

The impacts from emissions from biogas production and spreading of digestate are 0.4% higher than the avoided emissions from the substituted mineral fertilizer production in the RS. How- ever, in the AS the increased avoided fertilizer production, resulting from co-digestion of the OFHW at the manure and sludge-based biogas plants, exceeds the emissions from biogas production and spreading of manure by 3%.

The avoided production of fossil-based heat and electricity due to green energy production from the WtE as well as the biogas plants correspond to 13% and 7% of the total avoided impacts in the AS and RE respectively.
37: In order to assess the consistency of the results we present a sensitivity analysis for the impact category HT and TE according to the recommendation of the ILCD handbook (European commission, 2010). The results obtained from the ReCiPe LCIA methodology (Goedkoop et al., 2009) at midpoint level, was compared with the USEtox nested multimedia model (Rosenbaum et al., 2011) which includes the two subcategories of human toxicity endpoints; i.e. cancer and non-cancer.

For the human toxicity, HT non-cancer, and the Terrestrial Ecotoxicity, TE, results obtained from the USEtox LCIA methodology (Fig. 4) is in agreement with the results obtained from the ReCiPe LCIA methodology (Fig. 3c and 3d). For both impact categories, zinc is the main contributor and the health impacts resulting from zinc emissions to soil via spreading of digestate are more influential compared to the avoided emissions. The increase in the volume of digestate spread due to the co-digestion of OFHW with a high content of zinc (Supplementary material, Table S8) is dominating the patterns in the results obtained from both the USEtox and the ReCiPe LCIA methodology.

40: The system described is a net producer of bioenergy able to decrease significantly the dependence of fossil energy. The green energy becomes more sustainable from a climate change mitigation and resource efficiency point of view moving from the reference to the alternative biowaste to resource management system. It should be mentioned that an assessment of the influence of future Danish energy system with an increased share of renewable energy is considered outside the scope of this study. Ultimately, the avoided emissions from substitution of fossil-based energy will zero in a future fossil-free energy system.

The WtE plant optimizes the energy production and delivers maximum capturing of pollutants through capturing and seques- tering of hazardous substances, mainly heavy metals, and phos- phorous contained in slags and fly ashes from waste combustion trough landfilling or used subbase layer in asphalted roads.

The biogas technology is able to extract energy and conserve valuable substances, e.g. N, P, K, in the biomass. This results in a reduction in the loss of valuable resources by turning waste into green energy and value added fertilizer production.

An increased emission of ammonia during spreading of natural fertilizers compared to mineral fertilizer is observed; however, not exceeding the avoided emissions from the production and use of mineral N fertilizers. As a result, we observe a reduction in marine eutrophication at the global scale. Similarly, for freshwater eutro- phication an increased substitution of fossil energy and avoided loss of phosphorous from the extraction of raw phosphate for mineral fertilizers production results in a reduction in the fresh- water eutrophication level at a global scale.

The modelled increased circular bioresource management in- dicates significant improvement of the efficiency in use of resources in biowaste. This is in agreement with a study supporting the use of biogas in Denmark as important alternative to the combustion of OFHW (Münster and Lund, 2010). Potential trade-offs from increased circular bioresource management systems lies in the relationship between fertilizer value (in terms of plant bioavail- ability of P) and quality (in terms of concentration of micro- pollutants). Therefore, from an environmental point of view we observe some challenges that call for further research and tech- nology development. To avoid an increase in environmental im- pacts, i.e. toxic impacts on ecosystem functioning (Jensen et al., 2015) and provisional services (Pizzol et al., 2014), upcycling technologies may be included in the value chain (Seghetta et al., 2016b).

In the system boundaries considered in this paper, environ- mental impacts related to production of manure are all allocated to livestock and not included in the system boundary. Although the emissions to air and soil during storage and spreading are included, the remaining indirect impacts are neglected and all allocated to livestock sector. However, the assumption is based on the fact that

the production of livestock would occur anyway. Therefore we as- sume that our system does not involve in an extra-production from livestock sector aimed to produce derived livestock products. 42: The system described is a net producer of bioenergy able to decrease significantly the dependence of fossil energy. The green energy becomes more sustainable from a climate change mitigation and resource efficiency point of view moving from the reference to the alternative biowaste to resource management system. It should be mentioned that an assessment of the influence of future Danish energy system with an increased share of renewable energy is considered outside the scope of this study. Ultimately, the avoided emissions from substitution of fossil-based energy will zero in a future fossil-free energy system.

The WtE plant optimizes the energy production and delivers maximum capturing of pollutants through capturing and seques- tering of hazardous substances, mainly heavy metals, and phos- phorous contained in slags and fly ashes from waste combustion trough landfilling or used subbase layer in asphalted roads.

The biogas technology is able to extract energy and conserve valuable substances, e.g. N, P, K, in the biomass. This results in a reduction in the loss of valuable resources by turning waste into green energy and value added fertilizer production.

An increased emission of ammonia during spreading of natural fertilizers compared to mineral fertilizer is observed; however, not exceeding the avoided emissions from the production and use of mineral N fertilizers. As a result, we observe a reduction in marine eutrophication at the global scale. Similarly, for freshwater eutro- phication an increased substitution of fossil energy and avoided loss of phosphorous from the extraction of raw phosphate for mineral fertilizers production results in a reduction in the fresh- water eutrophication level at a global scale.

The modelled increased circular bioresource management in- dicates significant improvement of the efficiency in use of resources in biowaste. This is in agreement with a study supporting the use of biogas in Denmark as important alternative to the combustion of OFHW (Münster and Lund, 2010). Potential trade-offs from increased circular bioresource management systems lies in the relationship between fertilizer value (in terms of plant bioavail- ability of P) and quality (in terms of concentration of micro- pollutants). Therefore, from an environmental point of view we observe some challenges that call for further research and tech- nology development. To avoid an increase in environmental im- pacts, i.e. toxic impacts on ecosystem functioning (Jensen et al., 2015) and provisional services (Pizzol et al., 2014), upcycling technologies may be included in the value chain (Seghetta et al., 2016b).

In the system boundaries considered in this paper, environ- mental impacts related to production of manure are all allocated to livestock and not included in the system boundary. Although the emissions to air and soil during storage and spreading are included, the remaining indirect impacts are neglected and all allocated to livestock sector. However, the assumption is based on the fact that

the production of livestock would occur anyway. Therefore we as- sume that our system does not involve in an extra-production from livestock sector aimed to produce derived livestock products. 44: The system described is a net producer of bioenergy able to decrease significantly the dependence of fossil energy. The green energy becomes more sustainable from a climate change mitigation and resource efficiency point of view moving from the reference to the alternative biowaste to resource management system. It should be mentioned that an assessment of the influence of future Danish energy system with an increased share of renewable energy is considered outside the scope of this study. Ultimately, the avoided emissions from substitution of fossil-based energy will zero in a future fossil-free energy system.

The WtE plant optimizes the energy production and delivers maximum capturing of pollutants through capturing and seques- tering of hazardous substances, mainly heavy metals, and phos- phorous contained in slags and fly ashes from waste combustion trough landfilling or used subbase layer in asphalted roads.

The biogas technology is able to extract energy and conserve valuable substances, e.g. N, P, K, in the biomass. This results in a reduction in the loss of valuable resources by turning waste into green energy and value added fertilizer production.

An increased emission of ammonia during spreading of natural fertilizers compared to mineral fertilizer is observed; however, not exceeding the avoided emissions from the production and use of mineral N fertilizers. As a result, we observe a reduction in marine eutrophication at the global scale. Similarly, for freshwater eutro- phication an increased substitution of fossil energy and avoided loss of phosphorous from the extraction of raw phosphate for mineral fertilizers production results in a reduction in the fresh- water eutrophication level at a global scale.

The modelled increased circular bioresource management in- dicates significant improvement of the efficiency in use of resources in biowaste. This is in agreement with a study supporting the use of biogas in Denmark as important alternative to the combustion of OFHW (Münster and Lund, 2010). Potential trade-offs from increased circular bioresource management systems lies in the relationship between fertilizer value (in terms of plant bioavail- ability of P) and quality (in terms of concentration of micro- pollutants). Therefore, from an environmental point of view we observe some challenges that call for further research and tech- nology development. To avoid an increase in environmental im- pacts, i.e. toxic impacts on ecosystem functioning (Jensen et al., 2015) and provisional services (Pizzol et al., 2014), upcycling technologies may be included in the value chain (Seghetta et al., 2016b).

In the system boundaries considered in this paper, environ- mental impacts related to production of manure are all allocated to livestock and not included in the system boundary. Although the emissions to air and soil during storage and spreading are included, the remaining indirect impacts are neglected and all allocated to livestock sector. However, the assumption is based on the fact that

the production of livestock would occur anyway. Therefore we as- sume that our system does not involve in an extra-production from livestock sector aimed to produce derived livestock products. The WtE plant optimizes the energy production and delivers maximum capturing of pollutants through capturing and seques- tering of hazardous substances, mainly heavy metals, and phos- phorous contained in slags and fly ashes from waste combustion trough landfilling or used subbase layer in asphalted roads.

The biogas technology is able to extract energy and conserve valuable substances, e.g. N, P, K, in the biomass. This results in a reduction in the loss of valuable resources by turning waste into green energy and value added fertilizer production.

An increased emission of ammonia during spreading of natural fertilizers compared to mineral fertilizer is observed; however, not exceeding the avoided emissions from the production and use of mineral N fertilizers. As a result, we observe a reduction in marine eutrophication at the global scale. Similarly, for freshwater eutro- phication an increased substitution of fossil energy and avoided loss of phosphorous from the extraction of raw phosphate for mineral fertilizers production results in a reduction in the fresh- water eutrophication level at a global scale.

The modelled increased circular bioresource management in- dicates significant improvement of the efficiency in use of resources in biowaste. This is in agreement with a study supporting the use of biogas in Denmark as important alternative to the combustion of OFHW (Münster and Lund, 2010). Potential trade-offs from increased circular bioresource management systems lies in the relationship between fertilizer value (in terms of plant bioavail- ability of P) and quality (in terms of concentration of micro- pollutants). Therefore, from an environmental point of view we observe some challenges that call for further research and tech- nology development. To avoid an increase in environmental im- pacts, i.e. toxic impacts on ecosystem functioning (Jensen et al., 2015) and provisional services (Pizzol et al., 2014), upcycling technologies may be included in the value chain (Seghetta et al., 2016b).

In the system boundaries considered in this paper, environ- mental impacts related to production of manure are all allocated to livestock and not included in the system boundary. Although the emissions to air and soil during storage and spreading are included, the remaining indirect impacts are neglected and all allocated to livestock sector. However, the assumption is based on the fact that

the production of livestock would occur anyway. Therefore we as- sume that our system does not involve in an extra-production from livestock sector aimed to produce derived livestock products. 49: The system described is a net producer of bioenergy able to decrease significantly the dependence of fossil energy. The green energy becomes more sustainable from a climate change mitigation and resource efficiency point of view moving from the reference to the alternative biowaste to resource management system. It should be mentioned that an assessment of the influence of future Danish energy system with an increased share of renewable energy is considered outside the scope of this study. Ultimately, the avoided emissions from substitution of fossil-based energy will zero in a future fossil-free energy system.

The WtE plant optimizes the energy production and delivers maximum capturing of pollutants through capturing and seques- tering of hazardous substances, mainly heavy metals, and phos- phorous contained in slags and fly ashes from waste combustion trough landfilling or used subbase layer in asphalted roads.

The biogas technology is able to extract energy and conserve valuable substances, e.g. N, P, K, in the biomass. This results in a reduction in the loss of valuable resources by turning waste into green energy and value added fertilizer production.

An increased emission of ammonia during spreading of natural fertilizers compared to mineral fertilizer is observed; however, not exceeding the avoided emissions from the production and use of mineral N fertilizers. As a result, we observe a reduction in marine eutrophication at the global scale. Similarly, for freshwater eutro- phication an increased substitution of fossil energy and avoided loss of phosphorous from the extraction of raw phosphate for mineral fertilizers production results in a reduction in the fresh- water eutrophication level at a global scale.

The modelled increased circular bioresource management in- dicates significant improvement of the efficiency in use of resources in biowaste. This is in agreement with a study supporting the use of biogas in Denmark as important alternative to the combustion of OFHW (Münster and Lund, 2010). Potential trade-offs from increased circular bioresource management systems lies in the relationship between fertilizer value (in terms of plant bioavail- ability of P) and quality (in terms of concentration of micro- pollutants). Therefore, from an environmental point of view we observe some challenges that call for further research and tech- nology development. To avoid an increase in environmental im- pacts, i.e. toxic impacts on ecosystem functioning (Jensen et al., 2015) and provisional services (Pizzol et al., 2014), upcycling technologies may be included in the value chain (Seghetta et al., 2016b).

In the system boundaries considered in this paper, environ- mental impacts related to production of manure are all allocated to livestock and not included in the system boundary. Although the emissions to air and soil during storage and spreading are included, the remaining indirect impacts are neglected and all allocated to livestock sector. However, the assumption is based on the fact that

the production of livestock would occur anyway. Therefore we as- sume that our system does not involve in an extra-production from livestock sector aimed to produce derived livestock products. 51: The system described is a net producer of bioenergy able to decrease significantly the dependence of fossil energy. The green energy becomes more sustainable from a climate change mitigation and resource efficiency point of view moving from the reference to the alternative biowaste to resource management system. It should be mentioned that an assessment of the influence of future Danish energy system with an increased share of renewable energy is considered outside the scope of this study. Ultimately, the avoided emissions from substitution of fossil-based energy will zero in a future fossil-free energy system.

The WtE plant optimizes the energy production and delivers maximum capturing of pollutants through capturing and seques- tering of hazardous substances, mainly heavy metals, and phos- phorous contained in slags and fly ashes from waste combustion trough landfilling or used subbase layer in asphalted roads.

The biogas technology is able to extract energy and conserve valuable substances, e.g. N, P, K, in the biomass. This results in a reduction in the loss of valuable resources by turning waste into green energy and value added fertilizer production.

An increased emission of ammonia during spreading of natural fertilizers compared to mineral fertilizer is observed; however, not exceeding the avoided emissions from the production and use of mineral N fertilizers. As a result, we observe a reduction in marine eutrophication at the global scale. Similarly, for freshwater eutro- phication an increased substitution of fossil energy and avoided loss of phosphorous from the extraction of raw phosphate for mineral fertilizers production results in a reduction in the fresh- water eutrophication level at a global scale.

The modelled increased circular bioresource management in- dicates significant improvement of the efficiency in use of resources in biowaste. This is in agreement with a study supporting the use of biogas in Denmark as important alternative to the combustion of OFHW (Münster and Lund, 2010). Potential trade-offs from increased circular bioresource management systems lies in the relationship between fertilizer value (in terms of plant bioavail- ability of P) and quality (in terms of concentration of micro- pollutants). Therefore, from an environmental point of view we observe some challenges that call for further research and tech- nology development. To avoid an increase in environmental im- pacts, i.e. toxic impacts on ecosystem functioning (Jensen et al., 2015) and provisional services (Pizzol et al., 2014), upcycling technologies may be included in the value chain (Seghetta et al., 2016b).

In the system boundaries considered in this paper, environ- mental impacts related to production of manure are all allocated to livestock and not included in the system boundary. Although the emissions to air and soil during storage and spreading are included, the remaining indirect impacts are neglected and all allocated to livestock sector. However, the assumption is based on the fact that

the production of livestock would occur anyway. Therefore we as- sume that our system does not involve in an extra-production from livestock sector aimed to produce derived livestock products.
